findings.title: Hard timeout abandons the turn without stopping the subprocess
findings.description: On the second timeout, the code only posts a Discord warning and breaks out of the turn loop. The child process is neither interrupted nor killed, and the outer worker loop keeps running. That leaves the session attached to a still-busy subprocess, so later queued messages can be injected into a wedged tool invocation or interleave with late output from the timed-out turn.
findings.suggestion: Treat the hard-timeout branch as terminal for the session: send an interrupt/kill to the child, close the queue, and return `true` so the outer worker tears the session down instead of reusing the process.

findings.title: Biased select can starve queued messages and interrupts while stdout is busy
findings.description: `tokio::select!` is marked `biased` with `reader.read_line(...)` first. If the subprocess keeps emitting background progress/log lines, the worker will keep choosing stdout and may never service `queue_rx.recv()` or `interrupt_rx.recv()`. For a Discord bot this means user follow-up messages and stop requests can appear to hang behind a noisy background task.
findings.suggestion: Remove `biased`, or explicitly prioritize interrupts and queued user input ahead of background stdout. If background draining is needed, batch a bounded number of stdout lines before yielding back to queue/interrupt handling.

findings.title: Background notifications are sent one message at a time with all Discord API errors discarded
findings.description: The worker emits `channel_id.say(...).await.ok()` inside loops for assistant blocks and tool-result errors, and the same pattern is repeated in the active-turn background path. A verbose background task can easily hit Discord rate limits or permission errors, and because every result is silently ignored, the bot drops user-visible state with no retry, logging, or fallback.
findings.suggestion: Batch/coalesce background output before sending, and at minimum log send failures. For rate-limit safety, prefer a single summarized message per background event burst instead of one Discord message per content block.

findings.title: Production worker still panics on poisoned mutexes via `lock().unwrap()`
findings.description: `last_activity.lock().unwrap()` is used in the worker on the hot path, and the same pattern appears again later in the file. If any panic occurs while that `StdMutex` is held, the mutex becomes poisoned and the next update will panic the worker task, tearing down the session unexpectedly.
findings.suggestion: Handle poisoned locks explicitly, for example with `lock().unwrap_or_else(|poisoned| poisoned.into_inner())`, or switch to a lock-free timestamp/update strategy if this state is only used for bookkeeping.

summary: The refactor is structurally cleaner, but the worker still has a few production risks: timeout recovery does not actually stop hung subprocesses, the biased select can starve user control paths, Discord send failures are silently dropped, and the hot path still contains panic-on-poison mutex unwraps.
